bibliographic_data.title: Search versus Decision for Election Manipulation Problems
bibliographic_data.abstract: Most theoretical definitions about the complexity of manipulating elections focus on the decision problem of recognizing which instances can be successfully manipulated, rather than the search problem of finding the successful manipulative actions. Since the latter is a far more natural goal for manipulators, that definitional focus may be misguided if these two complexities can differ. Our main result is that they probably do differ: If integer factoring is hard, then for election manipulation, election bribery, and some types of election control, there are election systems for which recognizing which instances can be successfully manipulated is in polynomial time but producing the successful manipulations cannot be done in polynomial time.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper shows that for certain election manipulation problems, recognizing whether a successful manipulation is possible can be easy (polynomial-time), but finding the specific actions required to achieve success can be hard (not polynomial-time).
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use complexity-theoretic hypotheses and prove results under these assumptions. They show that if integer factoring is hard, then for election manipulation, bribery, and certain types of control, there exist election systems where recognizing successful manipulations is easy but finding them is not.
outcomes: Proved that the decision problem can be polynomial-time while the search problem cannot for some election manipulation problems under a complexity-theoretic hypothesis (P ̸= NP ∩coNP).
Identified specific types of election control actions where the search problem polynomial-time Turing reduces to the decision problem.

Reduced the number of distinct standard control types by showing that destructive control by partition of candidates and destructive control by run-off partition of candidates are identical in certain cases.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Most theoretical definitions about manipulating elections focus on recognizing which instances can be successfully manipulated, rather than finding the successful manipulative actions. This paper shows that these two complexities may differ: If integer factoring is hard, then for election manipulation, bribery, and some types of control, there are election systems where recognizing successful manipulations is easy but finding them is not. The authors prove this under a complexity-theoretic hypothesis (P ̸= NP ∩coNP) and identify specific cases where the search problem polynomial-time Turing reduces to the decision problem.
